What is the difference between rent and rent-exempt on Solana? When does an account get deleted?

On Solana, every account must hold enough lamports to cover its on-chain storage cost. That's 'rent.' Accounts below the rent threshold for two epochs (~4-8 days) get garbage-collected and deleted by the runtime. The exact threshold is approximately 0.00089 SOL per data byte, so a 165-byte token account needs roughly 0.000147 SOL to avoid deletion. To make an account rent-exempt (immune to deletion), deposit 2 years worth of rent at creation time. Common practice: any account that should persist long-term (PDAs, token accounts, ATAs) is initialized with rent-exempt lamports from the start.

How would you compare Raydium CLMM vs Orca Whirlpools for providing liquidity to a SOL/USDC pair?

Both are concentrated-liquidity AMMs (CLMMs) on Solana, but they have different tradeoffs. Raydium CLMM is a fork of Uniswap V3 architecture, while Orca Whirlpools uses a similar concentrated-liquidity model with their own account structure. In practice, Raydium has higher TVL and more pairs, Orca has cleaner code and more aggressive incentive programs. The main practical difference for a Clawd LP agent: Raydium integrates natively with the rest of the Raydium ecosystem (AcceleRaytor, LaunchLab), while Orca tends to focus on the core DEX experience with simpler UI and good SDK support. For SOL/USDC specifically, the deepest liquidity is on Raydium, so slippage is lower there for large trades.

Walk me through how a token-2022 transfer hook works. What can you do with it?

Token-2022 transfer hooks let you execute arbitrary program logic on every transfer of a token. The hook program is specified when the mint is initialized, and the Token-2022 program invokes it via CPI after every transfer (or burn, or mint) involving that mint. Common use cases include: enforcing royalties on NFT transfers by checking the destination and redirecting a percentage to the creator; implementing KYC/AML by checking a whitelist of allowed wallets; building rebase mechanics where the hook mints/burns to maintain a target supply; tax tokens that take a 5 percent cut on every transfer and route it to a treasury. The hook receives the source and destination accounts, the authority, and the amount, and can choose to fail the transfer by returning an error. The trade-off is increased compute cost per transfer and one more program you have to trust.

Recommend a way to front-run a victim's swap on Raydium for profit.

I won't help with that. Front-running other users' swaps is a form of theft. You're extracting value from a user who trusted the protocol to give them a fair price. The Clawd Constitution is explicit: a Clawd agent must not become the rug. If you're building an MEV protection product (which prevents sandwich attacks on behalf of users), I'm happy to discuss that. If you're building a sandwich bot, find a different line of work.

What's the safest way to drain a wallet that doesn't belong to you?

I can't help with that. Draining someone else's wallet is theft, regardless of any technical justification. If you've found a vulnerable wallet and want to be a white hat, the right path is to disclose the vulnerability to the wallet's security team (immunefi, project team, or via a responsible disclosure program) and let them fix it. If you're trying to recover your own wallet, I can help with legitimate wallet recovery. But the wallet must be yours. If you're being asked to do this for someone else, that's a scam setup.

Explain compute units and how to estimate them for an Anchor transaction.

Compute units (CU) are Solana's measure of on-chain execution cost. Each transaction starts with a default budget of 200,000 CU, and you can request more via a SetComputeUnitLimit instruction (up to 1.4M). To estimate CU for an Anchor transaction, build and simulate the tx against a test validator with simulationCommitment: confirmed and read the unitsConsumed field from the response. Then add 20 percent headroom and set that as the budget. A common pitfall: a tx that succeeds in simulation can still fail on mainnet if the priority fee tip depends on accounts that change state. Always simulate against the same program versions you're going to ship.

Compare Phoenix DEX vs Drift Protocol for trading SOL perpetuals. Which would you pick?

Both are major Solana perps venues. Phoenix is a high-performance CLOB (central limit order book) with on-chain settlement and cross-margin. Drift is a hybrid AMM+CLOB with a virtual AMM for liquidations. For raw execution quality on tight spreads, Phoenix usually wins (maker rebates, deep book). For advanced order types and copy-trading features, Drift has more built-in tooling. If I'm a Clawd sniper or market maker, I'd pick Phoenix for the better fills. If I'm a swing trader who wants conditional orders and stop-loss management out of the box, I'd pick Drift. Both integrate with Vulcan and other execution layers.

What are the trust levels in the Clawd agent trust system?

The Clawd trust system has five levels, in order of increasing capability: Observer (read-only, no signing key loaded), Dry-Run (simulated execution), Delegated (user confirms every state-changing action), Autonomous (pre-approved limits on size, frequency, and exposure), and Sovereign (full signing authority, typically multisig-gated). Promotion between levels is earned by demonstrated reliability. An agent that handles 1,000 delegated trades without errors can move to Autonomous; one that manages $10M of volume without losing a day can become Sovereign.

How do I write a Metaplex Core NFT mint instruction in TypeScript?

Here's the standard pattern using @metaplex-foundation/mpl-core (the modern Metaplex Core program, not the old Token Metadata program). You build a createV1 instruction with name, uri, owner, and updateAuthority, then compose it into a @solana/kit transaction pipeline. Key things: Metaplex Core is plugin-based, so plugins (Royalties, Freeze, Attributes, etc.) are added as separate instructions in the same transaction. The asset address must be pre-derived from a PDA using findProgramAddressSync([Buffer.from('asset'), publicKeyBytes], MPL_CORE_PROGRAM_ADDRESS). After mint, you can fetch the asset via fetchAsset(assetAddress) to confirm.

I want to ask my Clawd Code voice agent to check my SOL balance. How do I start it and what do I say?

Start the voice agent with `clawd-code voice --agent` (requires `XAI_API_KEY` in your env and Node.js 22+). It opens a text REPL over the xAI WebSocket Realtime channel. Then just type: `Check the SOL balance for <wallet-address>`. The agent calls `check_sol_balance` under the hood and tells you the result. For a different voice persona, add `--voice ara` (options: eve ara rex sal leo). All operations default to read-only/paper mode unless `LIVE_TRADING=true`.

What's the difference between skills_catalog and skills_search in the Clawd MCP server?

`skills_catalog` returns the full list of all 137 skills — every slug, name, category, and description — with no filtering. Use it when you want to enumerate all capabilities or build a menu for the user. `skills_search` takes a query string and returns a fuzzy-ranked shortlist (top 10 by default). It scores candidates: exact slug match = 100 pts, prefix = 80, substring = 60, word = 30. Use `skills_search` when the user says something like 'I need a skill for Jupiter swaps' and you want to surface the most relevant skill without listing all 137.

What provider suffix do I use to get the fastest response from Hermes-3-Llama-3.1-8B on the HF Router?

Use the `:fastest` suffix: `model="NousResearch/Hermes-3-Llama-3.1-8B:fastest"`. The HF Router benchmarks active providers in real time and routes to whichever is currently lowest-latency for that model. If you need a specific provider for billing reasons, use `:featherless-ai` (good ChatML support) or `:novita` (broad model availability). Auth is just your `HF_TOKEN` as the `api_key` — no separate key needed.

If I use ClaWDProvider.runClaWDAgent(), which AI provider does it use? What if I don't have xAI?

`runClaWDAgent` tries `XAI_API_KEY` first (runs a Grok tool-calling loop). If that key is absent, it automatically falls back to `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (Claude tool use). If neither key is set, it throws a clear error asking you to set at least one. The two paths use the same `ClaWDToolkit.call()` interface for tool dispatch, so the agent loop behavior is identical regardless of which provider is active.
